identity.title: Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat outsourced advice and implementation assistance on strategy, organization, financial planning and budgeting, records and office management, site selection, new-business formation, operational improvement, and related administrative or general management issues.
lens.excluded_activities: Internal corporate strategy teams, captive shared-service units, software-only products, staffing without substantive management advice, one-person practices without a transferable operating organization, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External clients buy expert capacity, independent judgment, analysis, recommendations, and change support through projects, task orders, retainers, or recurring managed advisory. Pricing may be hourly or time-and-materials, fixed price, cost reimbursement, performance based, or a hybrid; repeat revenue usually depends on relationships, specialization, and continuing transformation needs rather than mandatory subscriptions.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for broader NAICS 541600, not wage-weighted tasks for NAICS 541611, and the consulting experiment studies large-firm BCG consultants rather than lower-middle-market firms. I mapped research, data analysis, modeling, drafting, presentation production, project administration, knowledge retrieval, and first-pass recommendations into exposure; applied lower exposure to interviews, stakeholder persuasion, executive judgment, sales, and implementation; and excluded automation already embedded in templates and conventional analytics. The broader industry includes scientific and environmental field roles, which likely understates exposure for administrative management consulting, while selected experimental tasks and elite-firm workflows likely overstate exposure across the full current service basket.
a.rationale: Broader consulting employment is concentrated in management, business and financial operations, computer, market research, project management, sales, and administrative occupations. Management analysts gather information, analyze financial and operational data, develop alternatives, and present recommendations, all of which contain substantial digital work. In a randomized experiment, BCG consultants using GPT completed suitable knowledge tasks faster and completed more of them, but AI also reduced correctness on a task beyond its capability frontier. The task mix therefore supports high but not comprehensive current exposure. [S2, S3, S4, S5]
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study of NAICS 541611 firms that joins payroll by role to task frequencies, current automation, client-facing time, and validated AI feasibility would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The only public industry occupation table is for broader management, scientific, and technical consulting and includes work unlike administrative management consulting.
The BCG experiments involve a large, highly selective global consultancy and bounded tasks, not end-to-end lower-middle-market engagements.
The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and may overstate or understate the current labor intensity paired with this task share.

rho.bridge: Gallup measures AI use across US employees and organizations, while OECD surveys mostly medium and large AI-adopting firms in manufacturing and information technology and the experiments cover BCG consultants. I translate current use, demonstrated task gains, and the industry's digital work into five-year implementation of the exposed opportunity, then discount for client confidentiality, hallucination risk, weak workflow redesign, inconsistent proprietary data, review requirements, and smaller-firm change capacity. General workforce adoption likely understates consulting uptake, while large-enterprise and BCG evidence likely overstates lower-middle-market governance and integration capacity.
rho.rationale: Consulting work is already digital and the experiment shows that trained consultants can realize immediate gains on suitable tasks. US workplace AI use is widespread, but Gallup finds that workflow transformation lags individual productivity, and OECD reports lower adoption by smaller firms plus persistent skills and return-on-investment barriers. The five-year range assumes copilots become routine in research, analysis, drafting, and administration while high-stakes recommendations retain structured human review. [S4, S5, S6, S7]
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of lower-middle-market NAICS 541611 firms measuring production deployment, task coverage, accepted-output rates, labor hours, rework, client restrictions, and workflow redesign would materially change this value.
caveats: Reported tool use is not the same as implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Experimental productivity on bounded tasks may not persist across discovery, diagnosis, recommendation, and implementation.
Client data restrictions and errors that look persuasive can constrain implementation more than raw model capability.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently an external advisory business and the supplied firm count is already sized to established lower-middle-market operations, so most firms should satisfy the outsourced-service condition. Management analysts commonly work for clients on contract, and consulting relationships often generate repeat projects or task orders. I exclude practices whose revenue is isolated, founder-personal, nonrepeat, captive, or inseparable from an individual rainmaker, as well as software-only models. [S1, S3, S11]
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence on the supplied population showing repeat-client revenue, customer concentration, contract duration, delivery-team depth, founder dependence, and transferability would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
Repeat projects are not contractually recurring and may disappear when a principal leaves.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size classes and may include firms unlike today's normalized EBITDA population.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year ownership plans among US employer-business owners across industries, and Equiteq counts broader global specialty-consulting deals without an eligible-firm denominator. I use the employer-owner transfer intention as an anchor, check that consulting has an active transaction market, and discount gifts, internal succession, non-control recapitalizations, plans that never close, firms outside the frozen size band, and owner-dependent practices that wind down. Gallup's inclusion of all employer sizes and planned rather than completed transfers likely biases the anchor upward; unannounced private consulting transactions in Equiteq's data may bias visible deal activity downward.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while specialty-consulting deal activity remained substantial but became more selective. Eligible firms in this EBITDA band should be more transferable than microbusinesses, yet management consulting is unusually dependent on senior relationships and expertise. The base is below the stated general employer transfer intention because only completed qualifying control transfers count. [S9, S10]
s5.change_evidence: A five-year NAICS 541611 transaction census linking announced and private control changes to the eligible firm denominator, with seller EBITDA, buyer type, ownership percentage, and internal versus external succession, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup measures owner intentions across industries, not completed consulting-firm transfers.
Equiteq covers specialty consulting globally and does not provide the denominator required for a probability.
A sale can destroy client and employee value if relationships are concentrated in the departing founder.

q.bridge: Equiteq describes time-and-materials as the consulting industry's pricing foundation, BLS notes that self-employed analysts are commonly paid by the hour or project, and GSA documents multiple contract types for a federal vehicle that includes NAICS 541611. I assign low retention to hourly, labor-hour, and cost-reimbursement work; higher initial retention to fixed-price, retainer, and performance-based work; and then discount for rebids, renewal repricing, client scrutiny, scope expansion, and competitor adoption. The sources do not measure revenue weights: federal contracting may overstate formal pass-through, while the specialty-consulting report may overstate industry movement toward platforms and recurring services.
q.rationale: Hourly and time-and-materials engagements directly expose fewer delivery hours, and federal rules explicitly note that such contracts provide no positive profit incentive for labor efficiency. Fixed-price projects can preserve savings through the contract term, while recurring managed advisory and outcome pricing can retain more value. Equiteq also reports that AI-compressed timelines are making hourly billing harder to defend, so competition and client expectations should dissipate a substantial share of benefits within five years. [S3, S10, S11, S12]
q.change_evidence: A representative contract audit showing revenue by pricing model, renewal cadence, utilization treatment, post-AI scope and rate changes, realized project margins, and client concentration would materially change retention.
caveats: No public source measures the national contract mix or retained AI benefit for NAICS 541611.
Public-sector contract structures may differ materially from private consulting engagements.
Released capacity creates value only when firms can sell additional work or avoid hiring without impairing quality.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the management-analyst occupation across all industries, not constant-price service quantity for NAICS 541611, while Equiteq describes specialty-consulting market conditions and buyer preferences. I translate the positive occupational outlook and stated consulting demand into five-year service quantity, allow productivity to separate output from employment, and widen for economic cycles and discretionary project deferral. Employment growth can understate quantity when AI raises output per worker, while BLS's cross-industry occupation and Equiteq's transaction-market lens can overstate demand for the frozen general-consulting service basket.
d5.rationale: BLS projects management-analyst employment growth of 9 percent from 2024 to 2034 and expects organizations to seek consulting to improve efficiency and control costs, with especially strong growth at smaller specialist firms. Demand also benefits from transformation and operational complexity. The downside is meaningful because generalist consulting is discretionary, clients can defer projects in weak economies, and Equiteq reports a more selective market with better resilience in regulated and specialized work. [S3, S10]
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census revenue for NAICS 541611, combined with representative firm backlogs, proposal conversion, utilization, client budgets, and service-line mix, would materially change the year-five quantity ratio.
caveats: Occupational employment includes internal analysts and adjacent consulting industries and is not service quantity.
The outlook predates much of the latest AI-enabled client self-service and productivity change.
General strategy and transformation spending is cyclical, while regulated and implementation-heavy work is more durable.

o.bridge: BLS describes management-analyst duties, the experiments measure AI-assisted consultant performance, and Anthropic surveys AI users rather than measuring the share of consulting demand requiring an external lower-middle-market operator. I map interviews, contextual diagnosis, independent challenge, stakeholder alignment, accountable recommendation, sales, and change implementation to operator-required quantity, and map generic research, synthesis, drafting, and routine analysis toward client self-service or software supply. BLS may overstate operator need because it observes the existing occupation, while AI-user and elite-consultant evidence may overstate self-service because users are selected and experiments omit full client engagements.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve generic research, summaries, benchmarks, presentations, and preliminary analysis. However, BLS defines the work around gathering organization-specific evidence, interviewing personnel, developing alternatives, presenting recommendations, and checking whether changes work. The experiments show both strong assistance and persuasive errors, while Anthropic's management respondents identify judgment and management as capabilities AI lacks. This leaves a substantial but reduced role for accountable consulting operators. [S3, S4, S5, S8]
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted client study that tracks which current consulting scopes were renewed with human firms, internalized with AI, bought as software, or eliminated, split by task and client size, would materially change this share.
caveats: Management consulting has no universal licensing or statutory sign-off requirement that guarantees operator demand.
Routine generalist work is more exposed to client self-service than specialized, political, or implementation-heavy engagements.
The operator share depends on client trust, proprietary knowledge, and willingness to accept AI-generated advice, all of which can change quickly.

narrative.executive_view: Administrative management consulting contains a large wage-intensive layer of research, analysis, drafting, modeling, and project administration that AI can accelerate. The opportunity is constrained by a jagged reliability frontier, hour-linked pricing, client self-service, and the fact that diagnosis, persuasion, judgment, and implementation are central to what clients buy. Demand should remain broadly durable, but undifferentiated generalist work faces both economic cyclicality and structural pressure.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate desk research, knowledge retrieval, interview synthesis, financial and operational analysis, scenario generation, proposal drafting, report and presentation production, meeting documentation, and project administration. Experiments on consultants show meaningful speed and output gains on suitable work but worse decisions when models are applied beyond their competence. Firms need task routing, source grounding, confidential-data controls, expert review, and quality measurement rather than unrestricted use.
narrative.value_capture: Time-and-materials and labor-hour work convert efficiency into fewer billable hours and stronger customer demands for lower delivery cost. Fixed-price projects, retainers, recurring managed advisory, and outcome-linked fees retain more benefit initially, especially when released capacity can serve backlog. Over five years, rebids, client procurement, rival adoption, and easier self-service should share a large part of gross productivity with customers.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry is naturally composed of external service firms, and organizations in the frozen earnings band are more likely than microbusinesses to have teams and transferable client relationships. General small-business evidence indicates meaningful planned ownership transfer, and consulting deal activity is active. Still, founder rainmaking, tacit expertise, employee mobility, and client-change clauses can make a nominal firm difficult to transfer without revenue loss.
narrative.demand_durability: Organizations continue to buy help with efficiency, cost control, strategy, transformation, and organizational change, and BLS expects faster-than-average growth for management analysts. Smaller specialists may benefit as complexity grows. Demand is not protected by licensure and can fall quickly during budget cuts; generic studies and presentation-heavy work are especially vulnerable to internal AI tools, while regulated, specialized, and implementation-heavy mandates are more resilient.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Occupation data cover broader consulting, and direct lower-middle-market measures of AI deployment, contract mix, client retention, and ownership transfer do not exist. Persuasive model errors can damage client decisions, confidentiality failures can impair trust, and insufficient proprietary data can erase differentiation. An acquisition can also trigger departures by principals or consultants who embody the client relationship. Upside would come from disciplined workflow redesign, reusable intellectual property, and monetizing capacity into sustained demand rather than simply reducing hours.
narrative.principal_driver: Digitally produced analysis and deliverables account for a large share of high-cost work, and controlled experiments show that suitable consulting tasks can be completed materially faster with AI.
narrative.principal_weakness: Hourly economics and easy client self-service can pass productivity gains away, while the relationship-heavy remainder is difficult to automate and fragile in a control transfer.

sources.title: NAICS 541611 Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services
sources.supports: Defines the industry around operating advice and assistance on administrative and general management issues such as financial planning, records, organizational planning, site selection, start-up, and process improvement.

sources.title: Management, Scientific, and Technical Consulting Services Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Reports the broader consulting occupation mix, including management occupations at 16.21 percent of employment, business and financial operations at 33.12 percent, computer and mathematical occupations at 10.92 percent, and office support at 14.98 percent.

sources.title: Management Analysts
sources.supports: Describes information gathering, interviews, data analysis, solution development, recommendations, presentations, and implementation follow-up; reports 9 percent projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and common hourly or project payment for self-employed analysts.

sources.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
sources.supports: Randomized BCG-consultant evidence showing more completed work and faster performance on suitable tasks, alongside significantly lower correctness on a strategic task outside the AI capability frontier.

sources.title: GenAI Increases Productivity and Expands Capabilities
sources.supports: Reports a controlled experiment with 480 BCG consultants on coding, statistical, and predictive tasks, finding strong gains with AI on some tasks and risks from misunderstanding goals or inadequate supervision.

sources.title: Rising AI Adoption Spurs Workforce Changes
sources.supports: A representative survey of 23,717 US employees found half used AI at work at least occasionally, 28 percent used it frequently, and workflow transformation remained limited despite commonly reported individual productivity gains.

sources.title: The Adoption of Artificial Intelligence in Firms
sources.supports: Synthesizes firm-adoption evidence, finding generally low and uneven diffusion, lower use among small firms, weak use in core business processes, and constraints involving skills and uncertain returns.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index Report: Cadences
sources.supports: Reports observed AI-use and survey evidence from Claude users, including that management respondents were overrepresented but often identified judgment and management as capabilities AI lacks; explicitly warns the survey is not population-representative.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: A fall 2024 survey of 1,264 US business owners found that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and 5 percent planned to close.

sources.title: Management Consulting M&A Activity: Second Half 2025
sources.supports: Reports 166 specialty-consulting transactions in the second half of 2025, describes more selective buyer demand, identifies time-and-materials as the pricing foundation, and discusses uneven AI adoption and pressure on hourly economics.

sources.title: Human Capital and Training Solutions Ordering Guide Summary
sources.supports: Places NAICS 541611 in a federal management-consulting contract pool and documents fixed-price, cost-reimbursement, time-and-materials, labor-hour, hybrid, incentive, and performance-based task-order structures.

sources.title: Federal Acquisition Regulation Subpart 16.6
sources.supports: Defines time-and-materials and labor-hour contracting and states that time-and-materials contracts provide no positive profit incentive for contractor cost control or labor efficiency.
